content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission,
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444


June 13, 1978

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
Dobbs & Nielsen
Suite 2500, The Alcoa Building
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, CA  94111

Dear Chip:

     This is in response to your request for advice additional
to that contained in my letter dated May 31, 1978, concerning
the reporting of salary payments made to certain employees of
Eller Outdoor Advertising ("Eller").

     As I understand the situation you described, Eller will
identify specific elections for which it wants to encourage its employees to campaign and will allow campaigning
during paid work times.  For example, Eller may encourage its
employees to oppose a local ballot measure that will ban or
restrict the use of billboards.  Since the employees will be
rendering the services for a political purpose at the request
or direction of the employer, the salary payments will be
reportable by Eller if an employee spends more than 10% of his
or her compensated time in one month on services for a political
purpose.  2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423(a)(1).

     Although Eller wants to authorize employee participation
in specific campaigns, Eller does not want its employees, with
limited exceptions/, to exceed the 10% threshold of 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18423.  You have asked whether it is sufficient
for Eller to distribute a memorandum or other written notice
directing political activity but prohibiting employees from
spending more than 10% of their time on political work.

     The staff has determined that a memorandum or directive
alone is not sufficient.  Eller must also maintain written
records of the amount of work time its employees are engaged
in political activities.  The staff does not take a position


1/
     There will be higher-paid employees for whom Eller
will be reporting salary payments as contributions.

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.                                June 13, 1978
Page Two


     on the specific type of record that must be maintained.  Eiler
may adopt any method of recordkeeping which ensures that it
will be able to determine and report salary payments when an
employee exceeds 10% of his or her time on political work.

     I hope these comments are helpful.  If you have any
questions, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara Campbell
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Participants                    Date : June 8, 1978




From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Barbara Campbell Barbara

Subject: Eller Outdoor Advertising's Employees' Services as Contributions

       As you may recall, several weeks ago we considered a re-
quest from Chip Nielsen concerning the reporting of employee salary
payments as an in-kind contribution from Eller Outdoor Advertising
("Eller").

       As a follow up to my response, Chip Nielsen called with
the following additional information. Eller is going to get in-
volved in local elections where there is an ordinance or measure
banning bill boards. Eller will be identifying and selecting for which it wants its employees to campaign in opposition to
the measure. Therefore an employee will be rendering services for a
political purpose "at the request or direction of the employer" and
the salary payment will be reportable if an employee spends more
than 10% of compensated time in one month on such services. 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18423(a)(1).

       Eller is concerned about the steps it must take to ensure
that certain employees do not exceed 10% of their time on political
services. Eller will circulate a memorandum or other notice auth-
orizing employees to work up to 10% of their time (no more than 16
hours) on political work. Eller currently has no method of deter-
mining what its employees in the field (i.e. installers, repairers)
do. Is such a memorandum sufficient? Must Eller initiate time log
sheets?

       Incidentally, Eller will be reporting the payment of salary
to some of its higher paid employees who will be spending more than
10% of their time on campaigns.

_____________  At the June 12, 1978, Opinion Request meeting, the staff
BC:plh        concluded that a memorandum alone is not sufficient. Eller
               must maintain some kind of written record to ensure that
               its employees do not exceed 10% of their time on political
               work. (See letter to Nielsen.)

sections.question: Is it sufficient for Eller to distribute a memorandum or other written notice directing political activity but prohibiting employees from spending more than 10% of their time on political work, or must Eller maintain written records of the amount of work time its employees are engaged in political activities?
sections.conclusion: The staff has determined that a memorandum or directive alone is not sufficient. Eller must also maintain written records of the amount of work time its employees are engaged in political activities. The staff does not take a position on the specific type of record that must be maintained. Eller may adopt any method of recordkeeping which ensures that it will be able to determine and report salary payments when an employee exceeds 10% of his or her time on political work.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether an employer can rely solely on a written memorandum directing employees to limit political work to 10% of compensated time, or whether the employer must also maintain written records documenting actual time spent on political activities.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A memorandum alone is insufficient; Eller must maintain written records documenting actual employee time spent on political work. The FPPC does not mandate a specific recordkeeping method but requires that Eller implement some system to track and determine when employees exceed the 10% threshold for reportable in-kind contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both a formal letter (June 13, 1978) and a memorandum (dated June 8, 1978) regarding the same matter. Both portions provide consistent FPPC guidance. Citation to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423(a)(1) included.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is it sufficient for Eller to distribute a memorandum or other written notice directing political activity but prohibiting employees from spending more than 10% of their time on political work, or must Eller maintain written records of the amount of work time its employees are engaged in political activities?

CONCLUSION: The staff has determined that a memorandum or directive alone is not sufficient. Eller must also maintain written records of the amount of work time its employees are engaged in political activities. The staff does not take a position on the specific type of record that must be maintained. Eller may adopt any method of recordkeeping which ensures that it will be able to determine and report salary payments when an employee exceeds 10% of his or her time on political work.

Eller is going to get in- volved in local elections where there is an ordinance or measure banning bill boards.
embedding.summary: FPPC advises Eller Outdoor Advertising that while it may authorize employees to engage in political work up to 10% of compensated time, a memorandum directing this limit is insufficient without accompanying written records to track actual time spent on political activities.
